answer:
Top 3 Isolation Level Mistakes

Mistake 1: Using Default READ COMMITTED for Financial Operations

PostgreSQL's default isolation level (READ COMMITTED) allows non-repeatable reads — reading the same row twice in a transaction can return different values if another transaction commits in between.

-- ❌ BAD: READ COMMITTED allows double-spending
-- Transaction A reads balance = 10
SELECT leave_balance FROM employees WHERE id = 1; -- returns 10
-- Transaction B also reads balance = 10, deducts 8, commits
-- Transaction A deducts 7 (thinks balance is still 10)
UPDATE employees SET leave_balance = leave_balance - 7 WHERE id = 1;
-- Result: balance = -5 (overdrawn!)

-- ✅ GOOD: Use SERIALIZABLE for balance operations
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
SELECT leave_balance FROM employees WHERE id = 1 FOR UPDATE;
-- Row is locked — Transaction B waits
UPDATE employees SET leave_balance = leave_balance - 7 WHERE id = 1;
COMMIT;

Mistake 2: Not Understanding Phantom Reads

In REPEATABLE READ, new rows inserted by other transactions can appear in range queries (phantom reads).

-- Transaction A: count pending leaves for this month
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM leaves
WHERE status = 'pending' AND start_date >= '2025-03-01'; -- returns 5

-- Transaction B: inserts a new pending leave and commits

-- Transaction A: re-runs the same query
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM leaves
WHERE status = 'pending' AND start_date >= '2025-03-01'; -- returns 6!
-- Phantom read: a new row appeared within the same transaction

-- Fix: Use SERIALIZABLE to prevent phantoms
-- (PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ already prevents phantoms, unlike MySQL)

Mistake 3: Overusing SERIALIZABLE for All Queries

Setting SERIALIZABLE globally causes excessive serialization failures and retries, tanking throughput.

-- ❌ BAD: All queries use SERIALIZABLE
ALTER DATABASE mydb SET default_transaction_isolation = 'serializable';
-- Every read query now takes serialization locks!

-- ✅ GOOD: Use appropriate level per operation
-- READ COMMITTED (default): Most read queries, dashboards
-- REPEATABLE READ: Reports that need consistent snapshots
-- SERIALIZABLE: Balance deductions, inventory updates

// In Prisma, set per-transaction:
await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
  // Only critical operations use serializable
}, { isolationLevel: 'Serializable' });

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the overlap detection query ("does this employee already have approved leave on these dates?") uses REPEATABLE READ so the check and insert are consistent. The leave balance deduction uses SERIALIZABLE to prevent race conditions where two concurrent requests could over-deduct.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Mixing isolation levels in the same business operation. Checking balance at READ COMMITTED but deducting at SERIALIZABLE — the check can read stale data.

Mitigation: Keep the entire check-and-modify operation in a single transaction with the appropriate isolation level. Don't split it across separate transactions.

Definition

Isolation Levels define how much one transaction can see changes made by other concurrent transactions. PostgreSQL supports four levels: READ UNCOMMITTED (treated as READ COMMITTED in PG), READ COMMITTED (default), REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE. Higher isolation = stronger consistency but lower concurrency.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, different operations need different isolation levels: the leave list page uses READ COMMITTED (default, fast), the monthly report uses REPEATABLE READ (consistent snapshot for the whole report), and the leave balance deduction uses SERIALIZABLE (prevents double-deduction race conditions).

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Prisma: Different isolation levels for different operations

// READ COMMITTED (default) — fast dashboard queries
const leaves = await prisma.leave.findMany({
  where: { status: 'pending' },
  include: { employee: true }
});

// REPEATABLE READ — consistent monthly report
const report = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
  const totalLeaves = await tx.leave.count({
    where: { createdAt: { gte: startOfMonth } }
  });
  const byDepartment = await tx.leave.groupBy({
    by: ['departmentId'],
    _count: true,
    where: { createdAt: { gte: startOfMonth } }
  });
  // Both queries see the same snapshot
  return { totalLeaves, byDepartment };
}, { isolationLevel: 'RepeatableRead' });

// SERIALIZABLE — leave balance deduction
const result = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
  const emp = await tx.employee.findUnique({ where: { id: empId } });
  if (emp.leaveBalance < days) throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
  return tx.employee.update({
    where: { id: empId },
    data: { leaveBalance: { decrement: days } }
  });
}, { isolationLevel: 'Serializable' });

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Assuming PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ is the same as MySQL's. In PostgreSQL, REPEATABLE READ already prevents phantom reads. In MySQL, you need SERIALIZABLE for that.

Mitigation: Learn your specific database's isolation behavior. Test concurrency scenarios with two terminal sessions running transactions simultaneously to verify the behavior matches your expectations.

Top 3 Prisma Schema Mistakes

Mistake 1: Not Adding @@index on Foreign Keys

Prisma creates relations but doesn't auto-add database indexes on foreign key columns.

// ❌ BAD: Relation without index
model Leave {
  id         Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  employeeId Int
  employee   Employee @relation(fields: [employeeId], references: [id])
  // Missing @@index([employeeId])!
  // JOINs and WHERE employee_id = ? are slow
}

// ✅ GOOD: Always index foreign keys
model Leave {
  id         Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  employeeId Int
  employee   Employee @relation(fields: [employeeId], references: [id])
  @@index([employeeId])
}

Mistake 2: Using String IDs Without Understanding the Performance Impact

Using @default(uuid()) for primary keys when auto-increment would work. UUIDs are 16 bytes vs. 4 bytes for Int, and they fragment B-tree indexes.

// ❌ BAD for high-write tables:
model AuditLog {
  id String @id @default(uuid()) // 16 bytes, random = index fragmentation
}

// ✅ GOOD: Use autoincrement for internal tables
model AuditLog {
  id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) // 4 bytes, sequential = fast inserts
}

// UUID is fine for: public-facing IDs, distributed systems, API resources
model Leave {
  id       Int    @id @default(autoincrement()) // internal PK
  publicId String @unique @default(uuid())      // external reference
}

Mistake 3: Not Using Enums for Fixed Value Sets

Storing status values as free-text strings — no validation, easy to typo.

// ❌ BAD: String type allows any value
model Leave {
  status String @default("pending")
  // Nothing prevents status = "pnding" or "APPROVED" or "maybe"
}

// ✅ GOOD: Enum enforces valid values
enum LeaveStatus {
  pending
  approved
  rejected
  cancelled
}

model Leave {
  status LeaveStatus @default(pending)
  // Database-level constraint: only valid values allowed
}

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Prisma schema uses enums for LeaveStatus and LeaveType, composite indexes on [employeeId, status], and a unique constraint on [employeeId, startDate, endDate, type] to prevent duplicate submissions. These schema decisions prevented 3 categories of bugs before they reached code review.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Modifying enums in Prisma requires a migration that alters the PostgreSQL enum type. Adding values is easy, but removing or renaming values requires manual SQL.

Mitigation: Plan enum values carefully upfront. For values that might change frequently, consider using a reference table instead of a database enum.

Definition

Prisma Schema is a declarative file (schema.prisma) that defines your database models, relations, indexes, and enums. It serves as the single source of truth for your database structure — Prisma generates type-safe client code, migration SQL, and TypeScript types from this one file.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Prisma schema defines: Employee (with role-based access), Leave (with status enum and approval chain), Department (with manager relation), and AuditLog. The schema's type safety caught 12+ bugs during development — like passing a string where an enum was expected, or forgetting a required field.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// schema.prisma — HR Leave Management System
generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}

enum LeaveStatus {
  pending
  approved
  rejected
  cancelled
}

enum LeaveType {
  casual
  sick
  earned
}

enum Role {
  employee
  teamLead
  manager
  hr
}

model Employee {
  id           Int         @id @default(autoincrement())
  email        String      @unique
  name         String
  role         Role        @default(employee)
  departmentId Int
  leaveBalance Int         @default(20)
  department   Department  @relation(fields: [departmentId], references: [id])
  leaves       Leave[]
  approvals    Leave[]     @relation("Approver")
  createdAt    DateTime    @default(now())

  @@index([departmentId])
  @@index([email])
}

model Leave {
  id         Int         @id @default(autoincrement())
  employeeId Int
  type       LeaveType
  status     LeaveStatus @default(pending)
  startDate  DateTime
  endDate    DateTime
  days       Int
  reason     String?
  approvedBy Int?
  employee   Employee    @relation(fields: [employeeId], references: [id])
  approver   Employee?   @relation("Approver", fields: [approvedBy], references: [id])
  createdAt  DateTime    @default(now())

  @@index([employeeId, status])
  @@index([startDate, endDate])
  @@unique([employeeId, startDate, endDate, type])
}

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Running prisma db push in production. It applies schema changes directly without creating a migration file — no rollback possible, no history.

Mitigation: Use prisma db push only in development for rapid prototyping. In production, always use prisma migrate deploy with reviewed migration files committed to version control.

Definition

Migration Strategy solves the schema evolution problem — how to safely modify your database structure (add columns, rename tables, change types) in production without losing data or causing downtime. Migrations are version-controlled SQL scripts that transform the database from one state to another.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the team needed to add a leaveType enum (casual, sick, earned) to the existing leaves table with 10,000+ records. A naive migration would add the column as NOT NULL, failing because existing rows have no value. The correct approach: add as nullable, backfill existing rows with 'casual', then alter to NOT NULL.

Problems Solved

  • Team coordination: Every developer applies the same schema changes in the same order
  • Rollback safety: Each migration has a reverse (down) migration for undoing changes
  • Production safety: Migrations run in CI/CD pipelines, tested before hitting production
  • Audit trail: Git history shows who changed the schema, when, and why

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Step 1: Modify schema.prisma
model Leave {
  // ... existing fields
  type LeaveType @default(casual) // new field
}

// Step 2: Create migration
// $ npx prisma migrate dev --name add_leave_type

// Generated SQL (migrations/20250115_add_leave_type/migration.sql):
-- AlterTable
ALTER TABLE "Leave" ADD COLUMN "type" "LeaveType" NOT NULL DEFAULT 'casual';

// Step 3: For production with existing data, use a 3-step migration:
// Migration A: Add column as nullable
ALTER TABLE "Leave" ADD COLUMN "type" "LeaveType";

// Migration B: Backfill existing rows
UPDATE "Leave" SET "type" = 'casual' WHERE "type" IS NULL;

// Migration C: Set NOT NULL constraint
ALTER TABLE "Leave" ALTER COLUMN "type" SET NOT NULL;
ALTER TABLE "Leave" ALTER COLUMN "type" SET DEFAULT 'casual';

// Deploy: npx prisma migrate deploy (runs in CI/CD)

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Running destructive migrations (DROP COLUMN, DROP TABLE) without backup. One wrong migration can permanently delete production data.

Mitigation: Always backup before destructive migrations: pg_dump -Fc mydb > backup.dump. In Prisma, review the generated SQL before applying. Use prisma migrate diff to preview changes without applying them.

Top 3 Migration Strategy Mistakes

Mistake 1: Running Migrations Without Testing on Production-Size Data

A migration that runs in 100ms on 1,000 rows might lock the table for 10 minutes on 1M rows.

-- ❌ BAD: Adding a NOT NULL column with default on a huge table
ALTER TABLE leaves ADD COLUMN priority INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
-- On 1M rows: PostgreSQL rewrites the entire table (locks for minutes!)

-- ✅ GOOD (PostgreSQL 11+): Volatile default doesn't rewrite table
-- PostgreSQL 11+ handles ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT without table rewrite
-- But for older versions or complex defaults:
ALTER TABLE leaves ADD COLUMN priority INT;
-- Backfill in batches:
UPDATE leaves SET priority = 0 WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 10000;
UPDATE leaves SET priority = 0 WHERE id BETWEEN 10001 AND 20000;
-- Then add constraint:
ALTER TABLE leaves ALTER COLUMN priority SET NOT NULL;

Mistake 2: Not Making Migrations Backward-Compatible

Renaming a column that the running application still references. The deploy fails mid-rollout.

-- ❌ BAD: Rename column while old code still runs
ALTER TABLE employees RENAME COLUMN name TO full_name;
-- Old app version: SELECT name FROM employees → ERROR: column "name" does not exist

-- ✅ GOOD: Expand-contract pattern (3 deploys)
-- Deploy 1: Add new column, copy data
ALTER TABLE employees ADD COLUMN full_name TEXT;
UPDATE employees SET full_name = name;
-- Deploy 2: App reads from full_name, writes to both
-- Deploy 3: Drop old column
ALTER TABLE employees DROP COLUMN name;

Mistake 3: Editing Already-Applied Migrations

Modifying a migration file after it's been applied to any database. Prisma tracks migrations by name — modifying one causes a checksum mismatch.

# ❌ BAD: Editing an existing migration file
# "migration checksum mismatch" error on next migrate

# ✅ GOOD: Create a new migration to fix issues
npx prisma migrate dev --name fix_column_type

# If you need to start fresh in development:
npx prisma migrate reset  # drops DB + re-applies all migrations

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, a migration to add the approvedBy column initially used NOT NULL. This failed because existing leaves had no approver. The fix: add as nullable (Int? in Prisma), which maps to ALTER TABLE leaves ADD COLUMN approved_by INT — no data backfill needed since pending leaves legitimately have no approver.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Running prisma migrate reset in production. It drops the entire database and re-creates it.

Mitigation: Only use migrate reset in development. In production, always use prisma migrate deploy, which only applies pending migrations without dropping data.
